tt-umd CI — Public vs Private Build Runner

Should we move the build jobs off the self-hosted big runner onto public GitHub runners? Measurements, reasoning, and recommendations.

📅 Data window: 2026-05-01 → 2026-06-02 (29 active days) 📦 Sample: 1,527 runs of the two workflows that use the big runner 🧱 12,451 tt-ubuntu-2204-large-stable build jobs measured

Executive summary

The self-hosted tt-ubuntu-2204-large-stable runner builds fast but is unreliable to acquire. Most of the time a slot is free (median wait 43 s), but each build workflow fires ~10 jobs at once at a small pool, so on busy days the wait collapses — p90 ~20 min, p95 ~29 min, worst observed 82 min, and on the busiest day (Jun 1) the median was 27 min. Public ubuntu-22.04 runners start in ~3 s, every time. The price is slower 4-core compilation, largely recovered with ccache and precompiled headers.

43s … 82m
big-runner wait: median … worst (unpredictable)
p90 30m → 16m
build-device total: big tail → public (flat)
~3 s
public queue, every run
The most useful framing — the “ttsim floor”. Every PR run already contains run-ttsim-tests (“Build and test ttsim with newest UMD”), whose pure runtime is ~21 min 27 s — it builds all of tt-metal and is the longest single job. So any build job that runs under ~21.5 min on a public worker is a free win: it finishes before ttsim, never extending the run — even without ccache. Every job we moved is well under it.

Goals of this investigation

1Reduce total CI time

Public is roughly break-even with the big runner on a typical day and ~5 min slower in the best case (4-core compile). The win is at the tail: it removes the 20–80 min queue spikes that happen whenever the pool is busy, and makes run time predictable.

2Reduce pressure on the big private runner

Free the scarce self-hosted pool for the work that truly needs it (ttsim, hardware tests). Because the moved build jobs finish under the ttsim floor, this offload of ~10–13 jobs/PR is essentially free — it doesn't extend any run.

How long do we wait for the big runner?

Queue time for only the tt-ubuntu-2204-large-stable build jobs we're moving (12,451 jobs over the 29-day window):

wait for a runner minmedianmeanp75 p90p95max
all large-stable build jobs 0s43s6m37s7m04s 19m42s29m01s81m44s

~50% of jobs start within 1 min and ~75% within 7 min — but ~10% wait over 20 min, ~5% over 29 min, and the worst was 82 min. Pure build work is only 3–8 min, so on bad days the wait dwarfs the work.

Runs per day vs. wait time (the variability is the problem)

Bars = build runs/day; lines = median & mean wait. On low-volume days the wait is near-zero; when volume climbs (May 25, May 27, Jun 1) the wait spikes to 20–32 min. You can't predict which kind of day you'll get — public runners remove that risk entirely.

What we changed (our approach)

  1. Runner swap — Build (umd_tests), Build umd wheel, Build device (×9 matrix), manylinux + release builds → public ubuntu-22.04. build-device's container image repointed from internal harbor.ci.tenstorrent.net to public ghcr.io (public runners can't reach Harbor).
  2. ccache on the build jobs (persisted via actions/cache), incl. an optional ccache for the wheel (CMake enables it only when TT_UMD_CCACHE is set and ccache is installed; CCACHE_BASEDIR/CCACHE_NOHASHDIR handle pip's temp build dirs).
  3. Precompiled header for heavy stable externals (<fmt>, spdlog/tt-logger, STL) + gtest. Gated to clang-tidy-off builds (clang-tidy can't read a gcc .gch).
  4. Forward-declaration / include cleanup — pruned ~80 #include "umd/device/…" lines from ~40 headers; fixed transitive-include consumers. Verified on clang-20 + gcc-13.
  5. Shell fix — build-device's container default shell was sh, so a bash [[ … ]] check silently built the heavy branch; defaulted to bash.

Result — total run time (build-device, a pure build workflow)

build-device is the clean apples-to-apples case (build only, measured on both runners over the full window):

build-device total run medianmeanp75p90max
Big worker (n=781, full period) 11m24s16m46s19m43s30m06s89m44s
Public (warm, all changes) ~16m~16m~16m~16m~16m
Read this honestly. On a typical day the big runner is faster (median 11m vs ~16m) because its many cores chew through clang-tidy; public pays a ~5 min 4-core penalty. Public’s value is the p75/p90/max columns: a flat ~16 min versus 20 / 30 / 90 min. It trades a slightly-slower good day for eliminating the bad days.

Per-job detail (warm, all changes)

Build jobBig workPublic work ccache hitUnder 21.5m floor?
Build umd_tests3m34s1m12s–6m66% (96% fully warm)yes — free
Build umd wheel4m19s~6m100%yes — free
Build device (per variant)7m24s10–14m90%yes
manylinux wheel5m09s8m16s—yes
ttsim (builds all tt-metal)~21m27sdoesn't fit 4-core—it IS the floor → keep on big
Why build-device stays ~10–14m even warm. It runs clang-tidy on every file. ccache hits 90% on the compile, but clang-tidy isn't cacheable and dominates on 4 public cores. ccache/PCH can't shrink this — the lever is a larger public runner or a separate clang-tidy job.

Per-job verdict

JobVerdictWhy
Build umd_testsmoveFinishes far under the ttsim floor → free offload; warm ccache makes it as fast as the big runner.
Build umd wheelmove100% ccache → ~6m, under the floor → free offload.
Build devicemove *~5m slower on a typical day, but removes the 20–90m tail and offloads ~10 jobs/PR. Watch: clang-tidy/4-core floor ~14m.
ttsimkeep on bigBuilds all of tt-metal; doesn't fit a 4-core public runner. It defines the run floor.

Conclusions, mapped to the goals

Goal 2 — reduce big-runner pressure: clearly achieved, effectively free. The moved build jobs all finish under the ~21.5m ttsim floor, so offloading ~10–13 jobs/PR to public workers extends no run. This holds even without ccache and is the strongest, lowest-risk outcome.
Goal 1 — reduce total CI time: a tail & predictability win, not a blanket speedup. On a typical day public is ~equal-to-slightly-slower than the big runner (build-device 16m vs 11m median). The benefit is removing the queue spikes (p90 30m / max 90m → flat ~16m) and making runs predictable.
Scope note — the real end-to-end bottleneck is elsewhere. A full PR run (with hardware tests) is gated by test-all waiting for scarce silicon (6u / loudbox / glx), ~85 min — not by builds. These changes improve build-feedback latency and free the big runner; they don't shrink the full test run. That lever is test sharding / hardware capacity.

Recommendations

  1. Move Build, Build umd wheel to public with ccache — free win (under the floor), and predictable.
  2. Move Build device to public for predictability + offload; accept the ~5m typical-day cost, or pair with a larger public runner if its ~14m matters.
  3. Keep PCH gated to clang-tidy-off builds; keep ttsim and hardware tests on their current runners.

Methodology: queue = started_at − created_at, work = completed_at − started_at from the GitHub Actions jobs API. Window 2026-05-01 → 2026-06-02; 1,527 runs of Build Device + Build and run all tests for multiple build types; 12,451 jobs filtered to tt-ubuntu-2204-large-stable; re-run timestamp artifacts (negative queue) dropped. Public figures from warm runs 26812621709 / 26812642845 on branch ci/build-runner-public-comparison with all changes; verified to compile on clang-20 + gcc-13. ttsim floor (~21m27s) = PR-run runtime of run-ttsim-tests / Build and test ttsim with newest UMD.
